museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The month the Nintendo 64 finally reached Europe, bringing Mario 64 into UK living rooms and turning cartridge prices into high-street conversation.
museum.curatorNote: March is one of 1997's clearest UK hardware months: the European N64 launch is the centre of the exhibit, with Turok, Doom 64, and Symphony of the Night providing wider platform texture.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Nintendo's 64-bit machine became a real British retail object.

items.title: Nintendo 64 launches in Europe
items.body: The N64 reached Europe and Australia with Super Mario 64, Pilotwings 64, and Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire as the launch shelf.
items.sourceNote: Centre for Computing History lists Europe and Australia launch as March 1, 1997 with three launch games.

items.title: Turok tests the early N64 identity
items.body: Acclaim's shooter became an important early third-party N64 showcase. It was violent, expensive, technically impressive, and often discussed through the console's fog.
items.certainty: verified North American date; regional timing may vary
items.sourceNote: Giant Bomb lists March 4, 1997; some sources use nearby dates.

items.title: Symphony of the Night launches in Japan
items.body: Konami's PlayStation Castlevania arrived in Japan. Western players would wait, but the import aura began here.
items.sourceNote: Castlevania release-date references list Japan as March 20, 1997.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: March is unusually concrete: launch games, a flagship shooter, and arcade energy all at once.

items.title: Super Mario 64
items.body: European launch title and the clearest argument for buying the machine.
items.sourceNote: Listed as one of the three European N64 launch titles.

items.title: Pilotwings 64
items.body: A slower, stranger launch companion: less mascot, more sensation of floating through early 3D space.
items.sourceNote: Listed as one of the three European N64 launch titles.

items.title: Tekken 3
items.body: Namco's arcade fighter hit internationally in March, a reminder that arcades still had a technical glamour consoles chased.
items.sourceNote: Tekken 3 references list arcade release in March 1997, with PlayStation home release in 1998.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The N64 made hardware conversation physical: analogue stick, four ports, cartridges, and a new controller shape.

items.title: The N64 controller as museum object
items.body: Three prongs, one analogue stick, and a feeling that 3D movement required a new hand position.
items.sourceNote: European launch and launch titles are verified; controller context is descriptive.

items.title: PlayStation as the established shelf
items.body: The N64 was new, but the PlayStation already owned breadth: cheaper CD-ROMs, demo discs, and a growing library.
items.sourceNote: Included as UK retail context around N64 launch.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: March magazine pages felt like instruction labels for a new machine.

items.title: N64 launch issue culture
items.headline: Mario 64, launch verdicts and cartridge debate
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed N64 magazine-style cover image standing in for March 1997.
items.curatorNote: The launch issue was both review and buyer's guide: it explained how to hold the future.
items.body: The launch issue was both review and buyer's guide: it explained how to hold the future.

items.title: Arcade pages still mattered
items.headline: Tekken 3 in the arcade bloodstream
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed arcade magazine-style cover image standing in for March 1997.
items.curatorNote: Even in a console launch month, arcades still looked like the sharper screen.
items.body: Even in a console launch month, arcades still looked like the sharper screen.
items.sourceNote: Tekken 3 arcade month is sourced; cover is a reconstruction.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Console excitement was local; PC connection stayed deliberate.

items.title: The launch was offline
items.body: The N64 arrived as a living-room object, not a network platform. Its social life was a sofa, not a server.

items.title: PC players kept logging in
items.body: Diablo and QuakeWorld kept the modem relevant in a month dominated by console retail spectacle.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: March 1997 is the sound of a new box being opened.

items.title: Mario made the argument
items.body: You could complain about cartridges until someone handed you the controller and let you run around the castle.

items.title: The price was part of the texture
items.body: N64 games felt substantial partly because they were expensive enough to require negotiation.

sources.label: Nintendo 64 European launch
sources.note: Used for March 1 launch and launch titles.

sources.note: Used for the March 1997 Turok anchor.
